AI-assisted reasoning is helping doctors revisit unsolved pediatric rare disease cases and identify new diagnostic hypotheses. (Picture: created with AI)

4 min read
Just now

--

OpenAI and Boston Children’s Hospital used a reasoning model to review pediatric cases that remained unsolved.
For a family that has spent years looking for a medical answer, a diagnosis helps explain what disease a child has, which specialists to consult, which tests to monitor, which risks to anticipate, and what information to look for. In rare diseases, getting to that name is often the central problem.
OpenAI published a study conducted with researchers from Boston Children’s Hospital and Harvard Medical School, in which a reasoning model was used to reanalyze 376 unresolved pediatric cases of rare genetic diseases. The system helped generate hypotheses that, after medical review and clinical confirmation, led to 18 diagnoses. The work was published on June 18 in NEJM AI.
The model produced evidence-based hypotheses for doctors, geneticists, and researchers to review. Then came expert review, additional testing, confirmation in a certified laboratory, and communication of the result by the clinical team. The study shows AI integrated into a controlled medical process.
Why These Cases Are So Difficult
Reading DNA is not enough to find a disease. Genetic sequencing can reveal thousands of variants, but it is often unclear which one matters and which one does not.
Doctors need to cross-check those data points with symptoms, family history, and what science knows at that moment. That is where the problem begins. In rare diseases, information changes quickly. A gene that meant nothing five years ago may be linked to a disease today. A variant that once seemed irrelevant may be reclassified. A case that had no answer may make more sense when reviewed with new knowledge.
That is where AI enters this study. It helps organize scattered information and propose clues for specialists to review.
How the Study Worked
The team worked with de-identified clinical and genomic information from 376 pediatric patients. These were cases that had already gone through previous analyses and still had no diagnosis.
For each case, researchers prepared a package with standardized symptoms, clinical notes when available, age, sex, and a filtered table of genetic variants. In most cases, they also had data from the child and both biological parents.
They then used OpenAI o3 Deep Research to propose the most likely molecular explanation. The model had to connect symptoms, variants, inheritance, and scientific evidence, and show why that hypothesis made sense.
Each candidate was reviewed by at least two members of the team. Disagreements were resolved by consensus. A hypothesis entered the final results only if it met several steps:

• Expert review

• Classification of the variant as pathogenic or likely pathogenic

• Confirmation in a certified laboratory

• Clinical return of the result to the family
What They Found
The study included four groups of cases:

• Neurodevelopmental conditions

• Rare neuromuscular diseases

• Early-onset psychosis

• Sudden unexpected death in pediatrics
The final result was 18 diagnoses in 376 cases, an additional diagnostic yield of 4.8%. The number may seem small, but it refers to patients who had already gone through previous reviews without getting an answer.
The breakdown was uneven:

• 10 diagnoses in 100 neurodevelopmental cases

• 4 in 61 neuromuscular cases

• 2 in 200 sudden pediatric death cases

• 2 in 15 early-onset psychosis cases
OpenAI notes that the early-onset psychosis group was small, so that percentage should not be interpreted as a general measure of effectiveness.
One interesting detail is that 7 of the 18 diagnoses were “rediscoveries.” Those diagnoses had already been established outside the local research workflow, but they did not appear in the record the team was reviewing.
Kyra’s Case
The study includes the story of Kyra, a patient who began showing muscle symptoms at age 9. Her mother noticed that she could no longer get as low in her karate stances. Later, she started moving more slowly in soccer and walking or running on her toes. Her pediatrician could not find a clear cause for her muscle weakness and referred her to a specialist. The search for a diagnosis took almost two decades.
Kyra was one of the four diagnosed cases in the neuromuscular group. The team linked her disease to a variant in the HSPB8 gene and diagnosed a form of myofibrillar myopathy, a disease in which abnormal protein structures accumulate in muscle fibers and contribute to weakness.
A genetic counselor from the Manton Center called her with the result around her 28th birthday. According to OpenAI, Kyra had relied on a ventilator and used a wheelchair since age 13, although her condition later stabilized. That diagnosis came late, but it came.
Clarification: this study does not support using OpenAI’s models to diagnose diseases or make medical decisions. AI worked within a process led by specialists, with clinical review and laboratory confirmation.
Sources
OpenAI — Using AI to help physicians diagnose rare genetic diseases affecting children
Boston Children’s Hospital — Children’s Rare Disease Collaborative
OpenAI — Boston Children’s uses AI to unlock new diagnoses
